results.possibleIdentity: Jerry Marks is an artist and pioneer in 3-D media and stereoscopic imaging whose career began in the late 1960s. He is closely associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. Marks was interviewed by E.A.T. staff Julie Martin and Claudio Badal on June 12, 1968, as part of the organization's efforts to document collaborations between artists and engineers. His work is characterized by the intersection of art and science, specializing in 3-D silk screen printmaking, virtual reality, and perception-based laboratory art. Among his notable public works is a 3-D mural project for the 28th Street subway station in New York City (referenced as "7 waves 4 twenty eight"), which utilizes the lenticular properties of glass blocks to create an illusion of movement for passing riders. Over his career, he has also worked on 3-D effects for music videos (including for the Rolling Stones), theatre sets, and exhibits for the New York Hall of Science.

results.possibleIdentity: Larry Boyce (1946–1992) was a prominent decorative artist and painter active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Originally from Detroit, he studied art at Oakland University in the late 1960s, where he was known for experimental work and painting on canvas. He gained widespread fame in the 1970s and 1980s as a champion of Victorian-style stenciled friezes and murals, famously traveling over 250,000 miles by bicycle with his art supplies to decorate historic buildings across the United States. His work is preserved in several archival collections, most notably the Jack Eyerly collection on Pacific Northwest Art at Willamette University, which contains records of his work from 1971–1975. This same archival collection includes materials related to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization and their 1970 'Symphony Ball,' suggesting a potential association with E.A.T. during his time in the Portland art scene.

David Boyce was a young artist active in the late 1960s who was associated with Jasper Johns, a foundational figure in the New York art scene and a close collaborator of Robert Rauschenberg (co-founder of E.A.T.). In 1968, David Boyce was commissioned by Johns to design the layout for an exhibition catalogue at a time when Johns was involved with major institutional projects, including the 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' exhibition at MoMA (1968). This exhibition was closely tied to the emergence of E.A.T., and Boyce's role in Johns' circle places him within the specific artist-technology collaborative milieu of that era.

results.possibleIdentity: Hans Haacke (born August 12, 1936, in Cologne, Germany) is a highly influential conceptual artist and a pioneer of 'institutional critique.' Living and working in New York City since 1965, he became prominent in the late 1960s and early 1970s for art that explored physical, biological, and eventually social systems. His early works, influenced by his time with the German Zero group, utilized elemental materials like water and air to demonstrate real-time processes, such as in 'Condensation Cube' (1963–65). By the late 1960s, his focus shifted toward exposing the political and economic structures of art institutions. Notable works from this period include 'MoMA Poll' (1970) and 'Shapolsky et al. Manhattan Real Estate Holdings, A Real-Time Social System, as of May 1, 1971,' the latter of which led to the cancellation of his solo exhibition at the Guggenheim Museum. Haacke was also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, having expressed interest in collaborating with the group and producing works like 'Ice Table' that aligned with their mission of merging art with science and technology. He served as a professor at the Cooper Union in New York from 1967 to 2002.
